title: The Jump List
subtitle: {key:Ctrl-O} / {key:Ctrl-I} — walk back and forward through your big jumps.
summary: Vim records every "big jump" you make in a per-window jump list. {key:Ctrl-O} moves backward through it; {key:Ctrl-I} (Tab) moves forward.

blocks.text: Every time you make a *big jump* — a search, a {key:G}, a {key:%}, a mark jump — Vim pushes the position you came from onto a list. {key:Ctrl-O} walks back through it; {key:Ctrl-I} walks forward.

blocks.label: Jump forward (= Tab in most terminals)

blocks.title: What Counts as a "Big" Jump
blocks.text:
Vim has a precise list. Pushed onto the jump list:

  • Search: {key:/}, {key:?}, {key:n}, {key:N}, {key:*}, {key:#}.
  • File jumps: {key:g}{key:g}, {key:G}, {key:H}/{key:M}/{key:L}, {n}{key:G}, {key::}{n}.
  • Bracket: {key:%}.
  • Marks: {key:'}, {key:`} (any mark, including special).
  • Switching files: `:e`, `:b`, `:tag`.
  • Several others (`(`, `)`, `{`, `}` are jumps; `+`, `-`, {key:Enter} are not).

Not pushed: {key:h}/{key:j}/{key:k}/{key:l}, {key:w}/{key:b}/{key:e}, {key:0}/{key:^}/{key:$}, scroll commands.

The jump list is **per-window**, capped at 100 entries. {key:Ctrl-O} walks back; {key:Ctrl-I} walks forward. Making a *new* big jump from the middle of the list truncates everything ahead of you (like browser history).

blocks.title: {key:Ctrl-I} and Tab Are the Same Key
blocks.text:
Terminals send Tab and {key:Ctrl-I} as the *same* byte (0x09). Vim historically treats them as one keystroke. So if you remap Tab in *normal mode*, you also remap {key:Ctrl-I} — including its jump-list-forward behavior. This catches a lot of plugin users by surprise.

GUI Vim and Neovim with `tui-input` distinguish them. In a terminal, you usually can't.

{key:Ctrl-O} is your safety net. After any sequence of jumps you can walk straight back to where you started. Combined with {key:'}{key:'} (line of last big jump), you have a two-key undo for navigation.

blocks.title: The jump list is a bounded ring buffer
blocks.text: Vim records every 'big' jump (search, {key:G}, marks, {key:Ctrl-O} reach) into a per-window history capped at ~100 entries with duplicate suppression on the top. {key:Ctrl-O} walks back through the list; {key:Ctrl-I} (Tab) walks forward. Adding a new jump while in the middle of the history truncates the forward portion — same semantics as a browser back/forward stack.
